subject: Git and GitHub
description: Git is a distributed version control system for tracking code changes. GitHub is a web-based hosting service for Git repositories with collaboration features.

questions.question: What is Git?
questions.answer: Git is a distributed version control system (DVCS) designed to track changes in source code during software development. Created by Linus Torvalds in 2005, it permits multiple developers to work on a project together without interrupting each other's changes. Git is especially popular for its speed and ability to manage both small and large projects capably.
wrongAnswers: Git is a centralized version control system
Git requires internet connection to work
Git is used only for storing database backups

questions.question: What is a repository in Git?
questions.answer: A Git repository is a file structure that stores all the files for a project. It tracks changes made to these files over time, helping teams work together evenly. Git can control both local repositories (on your own machine) and remote repositories (usually hosted on platforms like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket), allowing teamwork and backup.
wrongAnswers: A repository is only used for storing images
Repositories can only exist on remote servers
A repository contains only the current version of files

questions.question: What is the difference between Git and GitHub?
questions.answer:
Git:
- Version control system used to track changes in files over time
- Runs locally on your computer
- Can be used offline, operates locally on your machine

GitHub:
- Platform where Git repositories can be stored and shared
- Cloud-based service
- Requires an internet connection because it is hosted on the web
wrongAnswers: Git and GitHub are the same thing
GitHub is required to use Git
Git runs only on cloud servers

questions.question: What is the purpose of the .gitignore file?
questions.answer:
The .gitignore file tells Git which files and folders to ignore when tracking changes. It is used to avoid attaching unneeded files (like logs, temporary files, or compiled code) to your repository. This keeps the repository clean and focused on important files only.

Common entries:
- node_modules/
- .env
- *.log
- dist/
- .idea/
- build/
wrongAnswers: .gitignore tells Git which files to always commit
.gitignore only affects remote repositories
.gitignore is required in every Git repository

questions.question: What is a version control system (VCS)?
questions.answer: A version control system (VCS) records the work of developers coordinating on projects. It keeps the history of code changes, permitting developers to add new code, fix bugs, and run tests securely. If required, they can restore a past working version, ensuring project security.
wrongAnswers: A VCS only tracks who edited files
VCS cannot restore previous versions of files
VCS requires all developers to work on the same computer

questions.question: What is the git push command?
questions.answer:
The git push command is used to share local repository changes to a remote repository. It changes the remote repository with the recent commits from the fixed local branch.

git push <remote-name> <branch-name>

Example: git push origin main
wrongAnswers: git push downloads changes from remote
git push requires git pull to be run first
git push can only be used with SSH protocol

questions.question: What is the git pull command?
questions.answer:
The git pull command updates the current local branch with changes from a remote repository and combines it with the local repository. It is essentially a combination of git fetch followed by git merge.

git pull = git fetch + git merge
wrongAnswers: git pull only downloads files without merging
git pull separates fetch and merge operations
git pull cannot update branches automatically

questions.question: What does git clone do?
questions.answer: The git clone command creates a copy (or clone) of an existing git repository. It downloads all files, branches, and history, enabling you to start working on the project or contribute to it. With git clone -b <branch-name>, you can download and work on an individual branch of a repository.
wrongAnswers: git clone creates an empty repository
git clone only downloads the latest version without history
git clone requires GitHub account

questions.question: What are the advantages of using Git?
questions.answer:
Using Git provides multiple advantages:
- Assists teamwork by supporting multiple developers to work on the same project together
- Each developer has a local copy of the repository, improving performance and enabling offline work
- Free and widely supported
- Supports work on various types of projects
- Each repository has only one Git directory
- Distributed nature means no single point of failure
wrongAnswers: Git requires all developers to be online simultaneously
Git cannot work with binary files
Git is only suitable for web development projects

questions.question: What is the difference between git init and git clone?
questions.answer:
git init creates a new, empty Git repository in the present directory, while git clone copies an existing remote repository, containing all files and history, to a local directory.

git init - Creates fresh repository
git clone - Copies existing repository
wrongAnswers: git init and git clone do the same thing
git clone creates a new empty repository
git init copies an existing remote repository

questions.question: What is git add?
questions.answer:
The git add command marks changes in your project for the next commit. It tells Git which files to involve in the later update, making them ready to be saved in the repository. This is the early step in recording changes in the Git repository.

- git add <file> - Stage specific file
- git add . - Stage all changes
wrongAnswers: git add permanently deletes files
git add commits changes immediately
git add can only stage one file at a time

questions.question: What is git status?
questions.answer: The git status command shows the recent status of your Git repository. It tells you which files have changed, which ones are ready to be committed, and which ones are new and untracked. This helps you monitor your work's growth and see what changes need to be staged or committed.
wrongAnswers: git status shows all commit history
git status commits changes to repository
git status only works with remote repositories

questions.question: What is a commit in Git?
questions.answer: A commit in Git denotes a snapshot of changes made to files in a repository. It captures all the changes you have made to files—like additions, modifications, or deletions of files at a particular moment. Each commit has a unique message explaining what was done. This helps you track your project's history, undo changes if required, and work with others on the same project.
wrongAnswers: A commit only saves files without any metadata
Commits cannot be undone once created
A commit can only contain additions, not deletions

questions.question: What is the purpose of the git clean command?
questions.answer:
The git clean command is used to erase untracked files from the working directory of Git repository. Its motive is to clean up the workspace by deleting files that are not being saved by Git, achieving a clean state with only tracked files present.

git clean -n (preview what would be deleted)
git clean -f (force delete untracked files)
wrongAnswers: git clean removes all files including tracked ones
git clean -n actually deletes files
git clean requires the -d flag to work

questions.question: What is a 'conflict' in Git?
questions.answer:
Git usually manages merges automatically, but conflicts occur when two branches edit the same line or when one branch deletes a file that another edits. Conflicts cannot be automatically resolved and require manual intervention.

Conflicts arise when:
1. Two separate branches have changes to the same line in a file
2. A file is deleted in one branch but has been modified in the other
wrongAnswers: Conflicts occur when two developers push at the same time
Git automatically resolves all conflicts
Conflicts only happen with binary files

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'Index' in Git?
questions.answer:
In Git, the 'Index' (also called the 'Staging Area') is a place where alterations are temporarily stored before committing them to the repository. It permits you to select and prepare specific alterations from your working directory before properly saving them as part of the project's history.

Flow: Working Directory → Index (Staging Area) → Repository
wrongAnswers: Index is another name for the remote repository
The Index only stores new files, not modified ones
Files in the Index are automatically committed

questions.question: How do you change the last commit in Git?
questions.answer:
To change the last commit in Git, use 'git commit --amend' after making changes:

1. Make your changes
2. Stage them with git add
3. Run: git commit --amend
4. This replaces the previous commit with a new one

Note: Only amend commits that haven't been pushed to a shared branch!
wrongAnswers: git commit --amend deletes the commit entirely
git commit --amend can only change the commit message
git commit --amend is safe to use on pushed commits

questions.question: What is git checkout?
questions.answer:
The git checkout command helps you switch between branches or return files to a previous state in Git.

Modern alternatives:
- git switch - for changing branches
- git restore - to return files to their previous state

These commands are more intentional for their specific tasks, offering better clarity and capability.
wrongAnswers: git checkout creates a new branch
git checkout only works with remote branches
git checkout can only be used for branches, not files

questions.question: How do you switch branches in Git?
questions.answer: To switch branches in Git, use 'git checkout <branch-name>' to move to an existing branch. Alternatively, use 'git switch <branch-name>' in newer Git versions for the same objective. This permits you to work on different versions or features of your project stored in separate branches.
wrongAnswers: git checkout and git switch are identical commands
You can only switch to existing branches
git switch cannot be used to create new branches

questions.question: Name some popular Git hosting services
questions.answer:
Popular Git hosting services include:
- GitHub (largest platform, Microsoft owned)
- GitLab (includes CI/CD features)
- Bitbucket (Atlassian product)
- SourceForge.net
- Visual Studio Online
- Azure DevOps
wrongAnswers: GitHub is the only Git hosting service available
Git hosting services are not used for collaboration
All Git hosting services require paid subscriptions

questions.question: What are the different types of Git repositories?
questions.answer:
Git has two types of repositories:

1. Bare Repository:
   - A repository without a working directory
   - Typically used for sharing projects remotely
   - No files can be edited directly

2. Non-Bare Repository:
   - Includes a working directory where developers can modify and track files
   - Used for local development

To create a bare repository: git init --bare
wrongAnswers: Bare repositories are the only type of repositories
Bare repositories have a working directory
You can directly edit files in a bare repository

questions.question: How does Git handle file deletion?
questions.answer:
Git provides the git rm command to remove files from the working directory and the staging area.

- To remove a file from both working directory and staging:
  git rm <file_name>

- To remove a file from Git but keep it in the working directory:
  git rm --cached <file_name>
wrongAnswers: git rm cannot remove staged files
git rm permanently deletes all repository history
git rm --cached keeps files in the staging area

questions.question: How can you create an alias in Git?
questions.answer:
Aliases can be created to simplify Git commands using:

git config --global alias.<alias_name> '<git_command>'

Examples:
git config --global alias.st status
git config --global alias.co checkout
git config --global alias.br branch
git config --global alias.cm commit
wrongAnswers: Aliases can only be created using shell scripts
Aliases replace Git commands permanently
Aliases cannot be created for complex commands

questions.question: How do you rename a branch in Git?
questions.answer:
To rename the current branch:
git branch -m <new_branch_name>

To rename a different branch:
git branch -m <old_branch_name> <new_branch_name>

To rename on remote:
1. Rename locally: git branch -m old new
2. Delete old remote: git push origin --delete old
3. Push new: git push origin -u new
wrongAnswers: Renaming a branch updates all references automatically on remote
git branch -m can only rename the current branch
You cannot rename a branch that has been pushed

questions.question: What is the difference between git fetch and git pull?
questions.answer:
git fetch:
- Retrieves updates from the remote repository but does not merge them into the local branch
- Allows you to review changes before integrating
- Updates remote-tracking branches locally
- Safe operation that doesn't modify working directory

git pull:
- Fetches updates AND merges them into current branch
- Equivalent to git fetch + git merge
- May create merge conflicts
- Updates working directory immediately
wrongAnswers: git fetch and git pull are identical operations
git pull is safer than git fetch
git fetch does not update remote-tracking branches

questions.question: Explain Git rebase and when do you use it?
questions.answer:
The Git rebase is a process to combine alterations from one branch into another. It creates a linear history, avoiding merge commits.

Use it to:
- Clean up commit history
- Keep a project history sequential
- Make feature branches up-to-date before merging

Warning: Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared repository!
wrongAnswers: git rebase preserves merge commits
git rebase is faster than git merge
git rebase is always safer than git merge

questions.question: How will you create a Git repository?
questions.answer:
To create a Git repository:
1. Download Git on your system if not already installed
2. Create a project folder in the location where you want your repository
3. Open Terminal or Command Prompt and navigate to your project folder
4. Run 'git init' in the project folder
5. This will create a '.git' folder, indicating your repository is set
wrongAnswers: git init creates a copy of an existing repository
git init requires internet connection
git init can only be run inside an existing Git repository

questions.question: What differentiates between git remote and git clone?
questions.answer:
git remote:
- Manages connections to remote repositories
- Lets you add, remove, and view remotes linked to a local project
- Does not download any files

git clone:
- Creates a local copy of an existing remote repository
- Includes all files, branches, and commit history
- Allows you to start working immediately
wrongAnswers: git remote downloads repository files
git clone is a type of git remote command
git remote cannot list all connected repositories

questions.question: What are the benefits of using a pull request in a project?
questions.answer:
Pull requests offer multiple benefits:
- Code Review: Team members can review code before merging, ensuring better code quality
- Collaboration: Multiple developers can work on the same project and discuss changes within the pull request
- Version Control: Helps track proposed changes and keeps the main branch stable
- Teams can work on distinct parts of the system and later combine their changes
wrongAnswers: Pull requests are only for closing branches
Pull requests cannot be reviewed by team members
Pull requests require all tests to pass before opening

questions.question: What is a Git bundle?
questions.answer:
A Git bundle is a collective file that wraps all data from a Git repository, such as commits, branches, and tags. It acts as a handy approach for relocating a repository offline or sharing upgrades when network connection is not available.

To create a bundle:
git bundle create <bundle_file> <refs>

To use a bundle:
git clone <bundle_file>
wrongAnswers: Git bundles are compressed archives like ZIP files
Git bundles can only be created from bare repositories
Git bundles require network connection to use

questions.question: What are the advantages of Git over SVN?
questions.answer:
Advantages of Git over SVN:
- Distributed Version Control: Git allows every developer to have a complete copy of the repository, whereas SVN relies on a centralized model
- Faster Performance: Git is generally faster due to local operations, while SVN requires network communication for many actions
- Branching and Merging: Git offers lightweight and efficient branching, whereas SVN branches are heavier and more complex
- Offline Work: Since Git is distributed, developers can work offline, committing changes locally before pushing them
- Better Conflict Resolution: Git provides more advanced tools for resolving merge conflicts
wrongAnswers: Git and SVN both use distributed models
SVN has better branching than Git
Git cannot work offline unlike SVN

questions.question: What is git stash?
questions.answer:
The git stash command temporarily saves changes that are not yet ready to be committed. It allows developers to switch branches or work on something else without losing their progress.

Key commands:
- git stash - Save current changes
- git stash list - View stashes
- git stash pop - Reapply most recent stash and remove it
- git stash apply - Reapply stash without removing it
- git stash drop - Delete a specific stash
wrongAnswers: git stash commits changes to the repository
git stash pop is the only way to recover stashed changes
Stashed changes are automatically pushed to remote

questions.question: How do you revert a commit that has already been pushed and made public?
questions.answer:
To revert a pushed commit:
1. git checkout <branch-name> - Switch to the branch
2. git log - Find the commit hash to revert
3. git revert <commit-hash> - Create a revert commit
4. git push origin <branch-name> - Push the revert

This creates a new commit that undoes changes without altering history.
wrongAnswers: git revert modifies existing commit history
git revert is faster than git reset
git revert can only undo the last commit

questions.question: Explain the difference between reverting and resetting
questions.answer:
git reset:
- Moves the HEAD pointer to a previous commit
- Removes changes from the commit history
- Can modify staged and working directory changes
- Modes: --soft, --mixed, --hard

git revert:
- Creates a new commit that undoes changes from a previous commit
- Does NOT modify commit history
- Safer when working in a shared repository
- Preserves complete history with audit trail
wrongAnswers: git reset and git revert are the same thing
git reset preserves commit history while revert deletes it
git reset is safer for shared branches

questions.question: What is the difference between git reflog and git log?
questions.answer:
git log:
- Displays commit history of a repository
- Only shows commits in the current branch history
- Used to review past commits and changes

git reflog:
- Tracks movements of HEAD and branch changes
- Shows all references, even those not in branch history
- Can help recover lost commits
- Used to restore lost branches or commits
wrongAnswers: git log and git reflog show the same information
git reflog entries are permanent and never expire
git reflog only tracks pushed commits

questions.question: What is HEAD in Git?
questions.answer:
HEAD in Git is a reference to the latest commit on the currently checked-out branch.

Key points:
- Determines which commit and branch you are working on
- When switching branches, HEAD updates to point to the latest commit of the new branch
- If in a detached HEAD state, you are working on a specific commit instead of a branch
- Located in .git/HEAD
wrongAnswers: HEAD always points to the main branch
HEAD is a file that stores the current commit message
Detached HEAD state is the normal working state

questions.question: What is the purpose of git tag -a?
questions.answer:
The git tag -a command creates an annotated tag in Git. Annotated tags store additional metadata such as the tagger's name, email, date, and a message describing the tag.

These tags are useful for marking important points like software releases.

git tag -a v1.0 -m "Version 1.0 Release"

To push tags to remote:
git push origin v1.0
wrongAnswers: git tag -a creates a lightweight tag
Tags created with git tag -a are automatically pushed
git tag -a only works on the current commit

questions.question: What is the difference between HEAD, working tree and index?
questions.answer:
HEAD:
- A reference to the current commit or branch you are working on
- Located in .git/HEAD

Working Tree:
- The directory where your project files reside
- Contains the actual project files and unstaged changes
- Your local project directory

Index (Staging Area):
- A file that acts as a buffer between the working tree and the repository
- Holds changes that are staged and ready to be committed
- Located in .git/index
wrongAnswers: HEAD, working tree, and index are all the same thing
The Index contains all tracked files in the repository
Working tree is stored inside the .git directory

questions.question: How to resolve a conflict in Git?
questions.answer:
To resolve a conflict in Git:
1. Identify Conflict: Git will alert you during merge or rebase
2. Open the Conflicted File: Look for conflict markers like <<<<<<< HEAD, =======, >>>>>>>
3. Resolve the Conflict: Edit the file to keep your changes, the incoming changes, or both
4. Stage the Resolved File: Use git add <file>
5. Commit the Changes: Run git commit
6. Continue Rebase (if applicable): Use git rebase --continue
wrongAnswers: Git automatically resolves all merge conflicts
Conflicts only occur during git fetch operations
After resolving conflict markers, Git commits automatically

questions.question: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
questions.answer:
git merge:
- Combines two branches and creates a merge commit
- Preserves both branches' histories
- Best when you want to keep full branch history
- Common in collaborative environments

git rebase:
- Reapplies commits from one branch on top of another
- Rewrites commit history to make it linear
- Creates a clean, straight history without merge commits
- Useful for personal branches before pushing
wrongAnswers: git merge always creates a linear history
git rebase preserves the original commit history
git merge cannot create merge conflicts

questions.question: What language is Git written in?
questions.answer: Git is mainly developed using the C programming language. The core features and commands of Git, containing its data structures and algorithms, are implemented in C. This choice of language ensures efficiency, speed, and portability across distinct operating systems and platforms.
wrongAnswers: Git is written in Python
Git requires Java to run
Git is written in assembly language

questions.question: What is git diff?
questions.answer:
git diff shows the differences between files or commits:

- Compare working directory with staging area:
  git diff

- Compare staged changes with last commit:
  git diff --staged

- Compare two commits:
  git diff <commit1> <commit2>

- Compare two branches:
  git diff branch1..branch2
wrongAnswers: git diff shows only committed changes
git diff cannot compare two branches
git diff --staged shows unstaged changes

questions.question: What is a Git commit hash?
questions.answer:
A Git commit hash is a unique identifier (SHA-1) for each commit. It:
- Helps to reference and track specific commits in the repository
- Is 40 characters long
- Can be shortened to first 7 characters for convenience
- You can use it to checkout, reset, or revert to a particular commit
wrongAnswers: A commit hash is randomly generated each time
Commit hashes are the same across all repositories
A commit hash is only 10 characters long

questions.question: What is a detached HEAD state?
questions.answer:
A detached HEAD state occurs when you check out a specific commit rather than a branch. In this state:
- Commits are not attached to any branch
- They may be lost if not properly managed
- Git will warn you when entering this state

To avoid losing work:
1. Create a branch before making changes: git checkout -b new-branch
2. Or checkout a branch to save changes before leaving
wrongAnswers: Detached HEAD means Git is not working properly
Commits in detached HEAD state are never lost
You cannot make commits while in detached HEAD state

questions.question: How can you delete a remote Git branch?
questions.answer:
To delete a remote branch:
git push origin --delete <branch-name>

This removes the branch from the remote repository. The local branch remains unchanged.
wrongAnswers: git push origin --delete only deletes local branches
Remote branches cannot be deleted
Deleting a remote branch also deletes the local branch

questions.question: What is the purpose of git cherry-pick?
questions.answer:
The git cherry-pick command:
- Applies changes from a specific commit to the current branch
- Useful when you want to incorporate changes from a branch without merging the entire branch
- Helps maintain a clean history by including only selected changes
- Ideal for bringing specific bug fixes or features from one branch to another
- Creates a new commit on the current branch with the same changes
wrongAnswers: git cherry-pick requires merging branches
git cherry-pick deletes the original commit
git cherry-pick can only be used on the same branch

questions.question: What does git ls-files do?
questions.answer:
The command git ls-files lists all files that are currently tracked by Git in the repository. It:
- Displays the files in both the working directory and the staging area
- Helps identify which files are being tracked and ready to be committed
- Does not show untracked files
- Does not show files ignored by .gitignore
wrongAnswers: git ls-files shows all files in the repository
git ls-files requires the repository to be connected to remote
git ls-files cannot be used to verify staging

questions.question: How do you fetch all remote branches?
questions.answer:
Run: git fetch --all

This command fetches all branches and their latest changes from all remotes without merging them.

To also see all branches (including remote):
git branch -a

To switch to a remote branch:
git checkout -b <local-name> origin/<remote-name>
wrongAnswers: git fetch only downloads the main branch
git fetch automatically merges changes
git fetch requires write permissions on remote

questions.question: What is the Git object model?
questions.answer:
The Git object model consists of four major types of objects:

1. Blobs: Store the file data (the contents of files)
2. Trees: Represent directory structures and contain references to blobs (files) and other trees (subdirectories)
3. Commits: Store repository snapshots with author, time, and parent info
4. Tags: Store references to specific commits, typically used for marking important points like releases
wrongAnswers: The Git object model has three types of objects
Blobs store file metadata, not content
Commits directly store file contents

questions.question: What is a Git hook and how might you use it?
questions.answer:
A Git hook is a script that runs automatically at certain points in the Git workflow, like before or after a commit, merge, or push.

Use cases:
- Pre-commit: Check code quality or run tests before committing
- Post-commit: Automate tasks like notifications after a commit
- Pre-push: Ensure tests pass before pushing changes

Hooks are stored in .git/hooks directory.
wrongAnswers: Git hooks are stored on the remote server
Git hooks run automatically for all users by default
Git hooks can only be written in Python

questions.question: How do you handle large files with Git?
questions.answer:
Use Git LFS (Large File Storage) to handle large files:

1. Install Git LFS:
   git lfs install

2. Track large files:
   git lfs track "*.psd"

3. Commit and push as usual. Git LFS will handle the large file storage automatically.

LFS replaces large files in the repository with pointers, while the actual file content is stored externally.
wrongAnswers: Git LFS replaces Git entirely for large files
Git LFS stores files directly in the repository
Git LFS is built into Git by default

questions.question: What is a 'bare repository' in Git?
questions.answer:
A bare repository in Git is a repository that doesn't have a working directory. It only contains the Git data, such as branches, tags, and commits, without the actual project files.

Characteristics:
- No working directory (no actual files)
- Used for sharing changes between collaborators (remote)
- Stored typically in the .git folder format

Create with: git init --bare
wrongAnswers: Bare repositories have a working directory with files
Bare repositories cannot be cloned
Only bare repositories can be pushed to

questions.question: What is branching in Git?
questions.answer:
Branching in Git permits creating separate lines of development. It allows users to work on features or fixes separately from the main codebase.

Key benefits:
- Parallel development on multiple features
- Safe experimentation without affecting main code
- Easier integration of changes
- Supports various workflows (feature branching, Gitflow, etc.)
wrongAnswers: Branches are copies of the entire repository
There can only be one branch at a time
Branches cannot be merged together

questions.question: What is a Git submodule?
questions.answer:
A Git submodule is a repository embedded inside another Git repository. It allows you to keep track of an external repository as part of your project.

Submodules are useful when:
- Including external libraries or dependencies
- Keeping their history separate from main project

Commands:
git submodule add <url> <path>
git submodule update --init --recursive
wrongAnswers: Git submodules are identical to git clone
Submodules update automatically when parent updates
Submodules cannot have their own commit history

questions.question: How can you undo a commit that hasn't been pushed?
questions.answer:
We can use git reset to undo a commit:

- To keep changes in the working directory:
  git reset --soft HEAD~1

- To remove changes from staging area but keep files:
  git reset --mixed HEAD~1

- To remove changes from both the commit and working directory:
  git reset --hard HEAD~1

Warning: --hard option permanently deletes changes!
wrongAnswers: git reset deletes commits permanently
git reset --soft keeps all changes in working directory
git reset --hard is the safest option

questions.question: How do you squash multiple commits into one using Git rebase?
questions.answer:
To squash multiple commits into one:

1. Start an interactive rebase:
   git rebase -i HEAD~<number-of-commits>

2. In the editor, change 'pick' to 'squash' (or 's') for the commits you want to combine

3. Save and close the editor

4. Git will open another editor to combine commit messages. Edit the message and save.
wrongAnswers: Squashing commits requires deleting and recreating them
You can only squash the last two commits
Squashing commits loses the original commit messages

questions.question: What is the difference between git reset --hard and git clean -fd?
questions.answer:
git reset --hard:
- Resets the commit history
- Resets the staging area
- Resets the working directory to a specific commit
- Discards any uncommitted changes

git clean -fd:
- Removes UNTRACKED files and directories from the working directory
- Does NOT affect the commit history
- Does NOT affect staged changes
- Leaves tracked files untouched
wrongAnswers: git reset --hard and git clean -fd do the same thing
git reset --hard only affects the commit history
git clean -fd removes staged files

questions.question: How do you configure Git for a specific project?
questions.answer:
To set a specific user for a project:

git config user.name "Your Name"
git config user.email "your.email@example.com"

These settings apply only to the current repository (local level).

Levels of configuration:
- --system: Applied to every user and all repos
- --global: Applied to all repos for current user
- (default/local): Applied only to current repo
wrongAnswers: Git configuration only works with git config command
Global configuration applies to all users on the system
Repository-specific config overrides global config

questions.question: What is a Git reflog and how is it useful?
questions.answer:
The Git reflog tracks the history of changes to HEAD and branches, including changes that are not part of the commit history (e.g., resets, rebase).

It allows you to:
- Recover lost commits
- Restore deleted branches
- Understand the sequence of actions taken

Commands:
git reflog

Recovery example:
git checkout <commit-hash-from-reflog>
wrongAnswers: git reflog is part of regular commit history
git reflog only tracks commits on the current branch
git reflog entries are automatically deleted after 30 days

questions.question: How do you manage multiple remotes in a Git repository?
questions.answer:
Manage multiple remotes using:

- Add a new remote:
  git remote add <remote-name> <remote-url>

- View remotes:
  git remote -v

- Remove a remote:
  git remote remove <remote-name>

- Rename a remote:
  git remote rename <old-name> <new-name>

- Fetch from specific remote:
  git fetch <remote-name>
wrongAnswers: You can only have one remote per repository
Remotes are created automatically when cloning
git remote add copies all repository data

questions.question: How do you perform a Git bisect to find a buggy commit?
questions.answer:
To perform a Git bisect:

1. Start the bisect process:
   git bisect start

2. Mark current commit as bad:
   git bisect bad

3. Mark a known good commit:
   git bisect good <commit-hash>

4. Git will checkout a commit between them. Test and mark as good or bad.

5. Repeat until the buggy commit is found.

6. Stop the process:
   git bisect reset
wrongAnswers: git bisect requires manual testing of each commit
git bisect only works with the main branch
git bisect cannot find the first bad commit

questions.question: What is origin in Git?
questions.answer:
In Git, 'origin' refers to the default name offered to the remote repository from which the local repository was cloned. Origin is used as a reference to control fetches, pulls, and pushes.

Common usage:
git push origin main
git pull origin main
git fetch origin
wrongAnswers: origin is the only valid remote name
origin is a special Git command
origin is automatically created on every clone

questions.question: How do you recover a deleted branch that was pushed?
questions.answer:
To recover a deleted branch:

1. Use git reflog to find the commit:
   git reflog

2. Look for the commit where the branch existed

3. Recreate the branch:
   git branch <branch-name> <commit-hash>

Or if you recently deleted:
   git checkout -b <branch-name> <sha>

Alternative: Check GitHub/GitLab's branch recovery features.
wrongAnswers: Deleted branches cannot be recovered
GitHub preserves all deleted branches forever
git reflog cannot find deleted branch commits

questions.question: What consists of a commit object?
questions.answer:
A commit object consists of:

1. A set of files that represents the state of a project at a given point in time
2. Reference to parent commit objects
3. A 40 character string termed as SHA-1 name uniquely identifies the commit object

Additional metadata:
- Author name and email
- Committer name and email
- Commit timestamp
wrongAnswers: A commit object stores the actual file contents
Commits do not contain parent information
A commit object is the same as a blob

questions.question: What is the difference between git stash apply and git stash pop?
questions.answer:
git stash pop:
- Applies the most recent stash
- Removes the stash from the stash list after applying
git stash pop = git stash apply + git stash drop

git stash apply:
- Applies the most recent stash
- Leaves the stash in the stash list for future reuse
- You can manually remove it later with git stash drop
wrongAnswers: git stash apply removes the stash automatically
git stash pop and git stash apply are identical
Stashed changes are pushed to remote automatically

questions.question: What is the difference between a pull request and a branch?
questions.answer:
Branch:
- A separate version of the code
- Exists within a repository
- Used for parallel development

Pull Request:
- A process done when there is a need to put a developer's change into another person's code branch
- Initiates code review process
- Discussion and feedback mechanism
- Used for integrating changes from one branch to another
wrongAnswers: A branch is a discussion thread for code changes
Branches and pull requests are the same thing
Pull requests can only be created from local branches

questions.question: Why is Git considered easy to work with?
questions.answer:
Git is considered easy due to:

1. Branching Capabilities:
   - Sophisticated branching for multiple features
   - Easier merge option
   - Efficient workflow tracking

2. Distributed Development:
   - Every developer has a local repository copy
   - Easier to trace and locate data if lost
   - Improved scalability

3. Pull Requests:
   - Easier interaction among team members
   - Proper track of changes

4. Effective Release Cycle:
   - Wide variety of features
   - Faster release cycles
wrongAnswers: Git is difficult to learn and use
Git requires expensive licenses
Git cannot handle large teams

questions.question: What command helps know the list of branches merged to master?
questions.answer:
To get a list of branches merged to the current branch:

git branch --merged

This shows all branches that have been merged into the current branch.

To show unmerged branches:
git branch --no-merged

To delete merged branches:
git branch -d <branch-name>
wrongAnswers: git branch --merged shows all branches in the repository
Merged branches are automatically deleted
git branch --merged only works on remote branches

questions.question: What is best: Create an additional commit OR amend an existing commit?
questions.answer:
It is always advisable to create an additional commit rather than amending the existing commit because:

- Amend operation destroys the previously saved state of that commit
- If only the commit message changes, it's acceptable
- But if contents get amended, it results in loss of important information
- Over usage of git commit --amend can have severe repercussions

Best practice: Create new commits rather than rewriting history on shared branches.
wrongAnswers: Creating additional commits is always bad practice
Amending commits is safer than creating new ones
You should amend commits after pushing to shared branches

questions.question: What is the functionality of git ls-tree?
questions.answer:
The git ls-tree command returns a tree object representation of the current repository along with:
- The mode of each item
- The name of each item
- The SHA-1 value of the blob

Example:
git ls-tree HEAD
git ls-tree <branch-name>
git ls-tree -r (recursive, shows files)
wrongAnswers: git ls-tree lists all commits in the repository
git ls-tree only shows file names
git ls-tree cannot show files in subdirectories

questions.question: What does git annotate command do?
questions.answer:
The git annotate command annotates each line within the given file with information from the commit which introduced that change.

Syntax:
git annotate [<options>] <file> [<revision>]

This shows which commit last modified each line of a file, useful for:
- Tracking when changes were made
- Finding who made specific changes
- Understanding code history
wrongAnswers: git annotate creates new commits
git annotate only works on the main branch
git annotate cannot show who modified each line

questions.question: What is git revert vs git reset vs git checkout?
questions.answer:
git reset:
- Moves HEAD to different commit
- Potentially changes history
- Modes: --soft, --mixed, --hard

git revert:
- Creates new commit undoing exact commit changes
- Does NOT modify history
- Safe for shared branches

git checkout:
- Switches branches or checks out files from commit
- Puts you in 'detached HEAD' state for direct commits
wrongAnswers: git reset and git revert modify the working directory
git checkout can undo previous commits
git reset changes commit history while git revert does not

questions.question: What is the significance of git push --force-with-lease?
questions.answer:
git push --force-with-lease is safer than git push --force because:

git push --force:
- Force-pushes changes regardless of remote updates
- Can accidentally overwrite others' work

git push --force-with-lease:
- Checks if remote branch has been updated since your last fetch
- Rejects push if remote has new commits
- Prevents accidental overwriting of other developers' changes

Always prefer --force-with-lease over --force when force-pushing.
wrongAnswers: git push --force-with-lease is the same as git push --force
git push --force is safer than git push --force-with-lease
git push --force-with-lease cannot detect if remote has new commits

questions.question: How do you make an existing branch track a remote branch?
questions.answer:
To make an existing branch track a remote branch:

git branch --set-upstream-to=<remote-name>/<branch-name>

Or the shorter version:
git branch -u <remote-name>/<branch-name>

Example:
git branch -u origin/feature-branch
wrongAnswers: Tracking branches are created automatically on first push
git branch -u sets the remote for the current branch only
You cannot track a remote branch that doesn't exist

questions.question: How do you configure multiple project-specific settings?
questions.answer:
For multiple configurations, use git config with different levels:

- System-wide (all users):
  git config --system

- User-wide (all repos):
  git config --global

- Repository-specific:
  git config (default, no flag)

Alternatively, use includeIf for conditional includes based on repository path in .gitconfig.
wrongAnswers: Git config levels cannot be combined
System-wide config is the highest priority
Local config is the lowest priority

questions.question: How do you update a Git submodule?
questions.answer:
To update a submodule:

1. Navigate to the submodule directory:
   cd submodule-path

2. Fetch the latest changes:
   git fetch

3. Update to latest commit:
   git pull

Or update from parent repo:
git submodule update --remote --merge

4. Commit the submodule change in main repo:
   git add .
   git commit -m "Update submodule"
wrongAnswers: Git submodules update automatically with parent
Submodule changes are committed with parent repo automatically
git submodule update only works with git submodule add

questions.question: What are the levels in git config?
questions.answer:
Git config has three levels:

1. System-wide (--system):
   - Stored in /etc/gitconfig
   - Applied to all users and all repos

2. User-wide (--global):
   - Stored in ~/.gitconfig
   - Applied to all repos for current user

3. Local (default):
   - Stored in .git/config
   - Applied only to current repository

Priority: Local > Global > System
wrongAnswers: System config has the highest priority
Global config overrides local config
Git config only has one level

questions.question: How to remove a file from git index without removing from filesystem?
questions.answer:
To remove a file from git index (staging) without removing from filesystem:

git reset <file_name>

Or:
git rm --cached <file_name>

Then add to .gitignore:
echo filename >> .gitignore

This prevents accidentally staging the file again.
wrongAnswers: git reset and git rm --cached are the same
Removing from index also deletes the file from filesystem
Files removed from index are automatically added to .gitignore

questions.question: What is the functionality of git merge --abort?
questions.answer:
The git merge --abort command is used for:

- Stopping the merge process
- Returning back to the state before the merging occurred
- Cancelling an in-progress merge with conflicts

It essentially reverses the merge operation and restores the pre-merge state.

Note: Only works if merge hasn't been completed and committed.
wrongAnswers: git merge --abort completes the merge
git merge --abort only works after merge conflicts
git merge --abort requires the merge to be committed first

questions.question: How do you apply a patch in Git?
questions.answer:
To apply a patch:

1. Create a patch:
   git format-patch -1 <commit>

2. Apply a patch from a file:
   git apply <patch-file>

3. Apply and commit:
   git am <patch-file>

The git apply command stages changes, while git am applies and creates a commit.
wrongAnswers: git apply commits changes automatically
git apply and git am are identical
Patches can only be created from committed changes

questions.question: What are the factors for choosing between git merge and git rebase?
questions.answer:
Consider these factors:

Use merge when:
- Working in collaborative environments
- Merging feature branches
- You want to preserve complete history

Use rebase when:
- You want clean, linear history
- Personal branches before pushing
- Keeping feature branches up-to-date

Avoid rebase when:
- On public/shared branches
- Other developers have based work on your commits
- In open-source repositories
wrongAnswers: Always prefer rebase over merge
Merge is faster than rebase
Use merge for personal branches and rebase for shared branches

questions.question: What is the Gitflow workflow?
questions.answer:
Gitflow is a branching model for Git:

Main branches:
- main/master: Production code
- develop: Integration branch for features

Supporting branches:
- feature/*: New features
- release/*: Release preparation
- hotfix/*: Production fixes

Benefits:
- Clear structure for releases
- Parallel development
- Organized workflow
wrongAnswers: Gitflow is the only Git branching strategy
Gitflow requires all branches to be long-lived
Gitflow cannot be used with CI/CD pipelines

questions.question: What is the difference between fork and clone?
questions.answer:
Clone:
- Creates a local copy of a repository
- Connects to original (origin remote)
- Used for working on your own copy
- One-way operation

Fork:
- Creates a server-side copy on GitHub/GitLab
- Your own repository under your account
- Maintains link to original (upstream remote)
- Enables contributing to projects you don't own
wrongAnswers: Clone and fork do the same thing
Forking requires git clone command
You cannot contribute back from a clone

questions.question: How do you sync a forked repository?
questions.answer:
To sync a forked repository with upstream:

1. Add upstream remote:
   git remote add upstream <original-repo-url>

2. Fetch upstream:
   git fetch upstream

3. Merge changes:
   git checkout main
   git merge upstream/main

4. Push to your fork:
   git push origin main
wrongAnswers: Syncing a fork requires deleting and recreating it
You can only sync from your own fork
git pull is sufficient for syncing a fork

questions.question: What is git log and how do you use it?
questions.answer:
git log shows the commit history:

Common options:
git log -n <number>     # Limit commits
git log --oneline      # Compact format
git log --graph        # Visual branch graph
git log --author=<name> # Filter by author
git log --since=<date>  # Filter by date
git log -p             # Show diffs
git log --stat         # Show file stats
wrongAnswers: git log only shows commits on remote branches
git log -p shows only file names, not content
git log cannot filter by author or date

questions.question: What are Git hooks available?
questions.answer:
Git hooks include:

Client-side:
- pre-commit: Before commit is created
- prepare-commit-msg: Before commit message editor
- commit-msg: After commit message is created
- post-commit: After commit is complete
- pre-push: Before push happens
- pre-rebase: Before rebase happens

Server-side:
- pre-receive: On server before accepting push
- post-receive: On server after push is accepted
wrongAnswers: Git hooks are stored on the remote server
Git hooks run automatically for all collaborators
Git hooks can only be written in shell script

questions.question: How do you configure Git hooks for pre-commit checks?
questions.answer:
To configure Git hooks:

1. Go to the .git/hooks directory

2. Rename or copy the sample hook:
   cp pre-commit.sample pre-commit

3. Write your script for checks:
   #!/bin/bash
   npm test
   npm run lint

4. Make executable:
   chmod +x pre-commit

Or use tools like Husky for automatic setup.
wrongAnswers: Git hooks require installation of additional software
Git hooks only run on the server side
Git hooks cannot run tests automatically

questions.question: What is a Git bundle and how to use it?
questions.answer:
A Git bundle packages repository data for offline transfer:

Create a bundle:
git bundle create file.bundle HEAD master

Verify bundle:
git bundle verify file.bundle

Clone from bundle:
git clone file.bundle

List contents:
git bundle list-heads file.bundle
wrongAnswers: Git bundles are only for backing up the repository
Git bundles cannot be verified before cloning
Git bundles include the .git directory

questions.question: What is interactive rebase?
questions.answer:
Interactive rebase (git rebase -i) allows:

- reword: Change commit message
- edit: Stop and amend commit
- squash: Combine with previous
- fixup: Like squash, discard this message
- drop: Remove commit
- reorder: Change commit order

Start with:
git rebase -i HEAD~n
Where n is number of commits to modify.
wrongAnswers: Interactive rebase can only modify the last commit
All interactive rebase options are safe for shared branches
squash and fixup do the same thing

questions.question: How do you find which commit broke something after merge?
questions.answer:
Use git bisect for binary search:

1. Start:
   git bisect start

2. Mark current as bad:
   git bisect bad

3. Mark known good:
   git bisect good <good-commit>

4. Test and mark:
   git bisect good  # or bad

5. Git finds the culprit commit

6. Reset:
   git bisect reset
wrongAnswers: git bisect requires testing each commit manually
git bisect only works with the main branch
git bisect automatically fixes the bug

questions.question: What is the difference between --soft, --mixed, and --hard reset?
questions.answer:
git reset --soft:
- Moves HEAD pointer only
- Keeps changes staged
- Working directory unchanged

git reset --mixed (default):
- Moves HEAD pointer
- Unstages changes
- Working directory unchanged

git reset --hard:
- Moves HEAD pointer
- Removes from staging
- DELETES working directory changes
- DANGEROUS - cannot be undone
wrongAnswers: All reset modes delete the commit history
git reset --soft deletes changes from staging
git reset --hard is the safest option to use

questions.question: What is Git blame?
questions.answer:
Git blame shows who last modified each line of a file:

git blame <file>

Options:
- -L <start>,<end>: Limit to line range
- -e: Show author email
- -w: Ignore whitespace changes

Useful for:
- Understanding code history
- Finding who to ask about changes
- Tracking down bugs
wrongAnswers: git blame modifies the file contents
git blame only works on the main branch
git blame cannot show when changes were made

questions.question: What is git gc and when should you run it?
questions.answer:
git gc (garbage collection) optimizes the repository:

- Removes unused objects
- Compresses file revisions
- Packs loose objects
- Prunes unreachable objects

Usually runs automatically, but manually:
git gc

With aggressive optimization:
git gc --aggressive

Should be run periodically for best performance.
wrongAnswers: git gc should run after every commit
git gc removes committed changes
git gc cannot be run manually

questions.question: What is git show and what can it display?
questions.answer:
git show displays various Git objects:

Show a commit:
git show <commit-hash>

Show a file at a commit:
git show <commit>:<file>

Show a tag:
git show <tag>

Shows: author, date, commit message, diff, and file changes.
wrongAnswers: git show only displays commit messages
git show cannot display file contents
git show works only on uncommitted changes

questions.question: How do you create and apply tags?
questions.answer:
Create a tag:

Lightweight:
git tag v1.0.0

Annotated (recommended):
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release version 1.0.0"

Tag a past commit:
git tag -a v0.9.0 <commit-hash>

Push tags:
git push origin v1.0.0
git push origin --tags

List tags:
git tag
git tag -l "v1.*"
wrongAnswers: Tags are automatically pushed with git push
Lightweight tags are recommended for releases
You cannot tag a past commit

questions.question: What is the difference between merge and rebase workflow?
questions.answer:
Merge workflow:
- Preserves complete history
- Creates merge commits
- Can make history cluttered
- Safe for all branches

Rebase workflow:
- Creates linear history
- No merge commits
- Must be careful with shared branches
- Cleaner history

Best practice: Rebase local branches, merge when integrating to main.
wrongAnswers: Merge and rebase workflows are identical
Always use rebase for shared branches
Merge workflow cannot preserve history

questions.question: What are Git worktrees?
questions.answer:
Git worktrees allow multiple working directories from one repository:

Create a worktree:
git worktree add <path> <branch>

List worktrees:
git worktree list

Remove a worktree:
git worktree remove <path>

Useful for:
- Working on multiple branches simultaneously
- Testing features without switching
wrongAnswers: Git worktrees are the same as submodules
Worktrees require separate Git installations
Worktrees cannot be removed after creation

questions.question: What is git reflog vs git log recovery difference?
questions.answer:
git log:
- Shows commits in branch history
- Cleans up old entries after gc
- May not show deleted commits

git reflog:
- Tracks HEAD movements
- Includes all actions (commits, resets, checkouts)
- Shows entries even after gc
- Entries expire after 90 days by default

Use reflog to recover:
- Deleted branches
- Lost commits
- Undone operations
wrongAnswers: git log can recover rebased commits
git reflog entries are permanent
Rebased commits cannot be recovered

questions.question: How do you undo git rebase?
questions.answer:
To undo a rebase:

1. Find the state before rebase:
   git reflog

2. Look for 'rebase' entry:
   git reflog | grep rebase

3. Reset to that state:
   git reset --hard HEAD@{n}

Or if you know the previous HEAD:
   git reset --hard ORIG_HEAD

This restores the state before the rebase operation.
wrongAnswers: git rebase cannot be undone
ORIG_HEAD only contains the last command
git reflog does not track rebase operations

questions.question: What are the best practices for Git commits?
questions.answer:
Best practices for Git commits:

1. Atomic commits: One logical change per commit
2. Clear messages: First line <50 chars, then blank, then details
3. Present tense: "Add feature" not "Added feature"
4. Reference issues: Include ticket/issue numbers
5. Review before commit: git diff --staged
6. Don't commit generated files
7. Test before committing

Example:
Add user authentication

-implements login/logout functionality
- adds session management
- closes #123
wrongAnswers: Commits should include all possible changes
Commit messages should be in past tense
Code review is not necessary before committing

questions.question: What is git add -p and why use it?
questions.answer:
git add -p (--patch) stages changes interactively:

- Splits changes into individual hunks
- Allows you to choose which changes to stage
- Lets you split, skip, or edit hunks

Commands within patch mode:
y - stage this hunk
n - skip this hunk
s - split into smaller hunks
e - manually edit hunk

Useful for staging only relevant parts of changes.
wrongAnswers: git add -p stages entire files only
git add -p cannot split hunks
git add -p is faster than git add

questions.question: What is git clean -n vs git clean -f?
questions.answer:
git clean -n (--dry-run):
- Shows what would be deleted
- Does NOT actually delete files
- Safe preview before cleaning

git clean -f (--force):
- Actually deletes untracked files
- Requires -f flag for safety

git clean -fd:
- Deletes untracked files AND directories

Never deletes files ignored by .gitignore (unless -x).
wrongAnswers: git clean -n actually deletes files
git clean -f is safe without the -n preview
git clean removes tracked files

questions.question: What is shallow clone and when to use it?
questions.answer:
Shallow clone downloads only recent history:

git clone --depth 1 <url>

Benefits:
- Faster download
- Less disk space
- Faster clone

Limitations:
- No full history
- Cannot push/pull all branches
- Cannot merge from upstream

Use when:
- You only need recent code
- CI/CD pipelines
- Quick testing
wrongAnswers: Shallow clone downloads complete repository history
Shallow clone is the default Git behavior
git clone --depth 1 has no limitations

questions.question: How do you compress a Git repository?
questions.answer:
To compress and optimize a repository:

1. Garbage collection:
   git gc

2. Aggressive packing:
   git gc --aggressive

3. Prune unreachable objects:
   git prune

4. Verify repository:
   git fsck

This reduces repository size and improves performance.
wrongAnswers: Compression is automatic and cannot be run manually
git gc deletes commits from history
Repository compression removes all branch history

questions.question: What is git update-index and when to use it?
questions.answer:
git update-index modifies the index to control file attributes:

Assume unchanged:
git update-index --assume-unchanged <file>

Undo:
git update-index --no-assume-unchanged <file>

Skip worktree (alternative):
git update-index --skip-worktree <file>

Use when:
- Large files that change locally
- IDE files that shouldn't be tracked
- Performance optimization
wrongAnswers: git update-index commits files to repository
assume-unchanged prevents files from being tracked
git update-index only works with new files

questions.question: What are the differences between git stash and git cherry-pick?
questions.answer:
git stash:
- Temporarily saves uncommitted changes
- Changes stay on your machine
- Can be applied anytime
- Multiple stashes possible
- For switching contexts

git cherry-pick:
- Applies specific commits to current branch
- Creates new commits
- Works with committed changes
- For applying bug fixes to multiple branches
wrongAnswers: git stash and git cherry-pick work the same way
git stash saves changes to remote repository
git cherry-pick saves changes locally

questions.question: What is git archive?
questions.answer:
git archive creates archive files of repository content:

Create zip of main branch:
git archive -o archive.zip HEAD

Create tar.gz of specific commit:
git archive --format=tar.gz -o snapshot.tar.gz v1.0

Create archive of subdirectory:
git archive -o partial.zip HEAD:path/to/dir

Does not include .git directory.
wrongAnswers: git archive includes the .git directory
git archive requires network connection
git archive can only create ZIP files

questions.question: What is git rev-parse and git rev-list?
questions.answer:
git rev-parse:
- Converts refs to SHA-1 hashes
- Resolves symbolic refs
- Get current branch name:
  git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD

git rev-list:
- Lists commit objects in reverse chronological order
- Count commits:
  git rev-list --count HEAD
- Find common ancestor:
  git rev-list --merge-base branch1 branch2
wrongAnswers: git rev-parse and git rev-list are the same
git rev-parse creates new commits
git rev-list cannot count commits

questions.question: What are Git alternatives and why use Git?
questions.answer:
Git alternatives:
- Mercurial (Hg): Similar DVCS, simpler
- Subversion (SVN): Centralized VCS
- Perforce: Enterprise VCS
- Fossil: Integrated bug tracking

Why Git is preferred:
- Fast and efficient
- Free and open source
- Excellent branching/merging
- Huge ecosystem
- Industry standard
- Great hosting platforms (GitHub, GitLab)
wrongAnswers: Git is the only version control system
Git requires expensive licensing
Git has poor branching capabilities

questions.question: What is GitHub Actions and how does it integrate with Git?
questions.answer:
GitHub Actions is CI/CD built into GitHub:

- Triggers on pushes, PRs, schedules
- YAML-based workflow files in .github/workflows
- Runs jobs in virtual machines
- Supports Docker containers

Example trigger:
on: [push, pull_request]

Benefits:
- Integrated with repository
- Free tier available
- Marketplace for actions
- Automatic testing and deployment
wrongAnswers: GitHub Actions requires Jenkins to run
GitHub Actions only works with GitHub Enterprise
GitHub Actions cannot deploy to non-AWS cloud providers
